perturbation.original_text: 2.10 "Improvement" or "Improvements" means any modification of a process or other technology described in a Patent, and any modification to Technical Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.10 "Improvement" or "Improvements" means any major modification of a process or other technology described in a Patent, that fundamentally alters its core functionality, and any significant modification to Technical Information that enhances its commercial value.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a higher threshold for what constitutes an 'Improvement.' The original definition is broad, encompassing 'any modification.' The modified definition narrows it to 'major modification' that 'fundamentally alters its core functionality' and 'significant modification' that 'enhances its commercial value.' This creates a contradiction because Article 5.00 refers to 'Improvements' and uses the broader term from Article 2.10 of the IP Agreement. Now some items under article 5.00 may not be granted with the license provisions set forth in Article III due to not meeting the updated conditions introduced in the new definition of the term `Improvement` or `Improvements`
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.00 Improvements made or acquired solely by the Company ("Company Improvements") during the term of this IP Agreement shall be deemed Technical Information hereunder and shall be subject to the license provisions set forth in Article III for Technical Information. Any patent applications and any patents relating to any Company Improvements shall be deemed Patents hereunder and shall be subject to the license provisions set forth in Article III for Patents.
perturbation.justification: The original definition of 'Improvement' was broad ('any modification'). The changed definition narrows it significantly ('major modification' that fundamentally alters functionality, and 'significant modification' that enhances commercial value). Article 5.00 states that all 'Company Improvements' are subject to license provisions in Article III. Because the definition of 'Improvement' has been restricted, certain modifications that would have been considered improvements under the original definition, and therefore subject to Article III licensing under Article 5.00, no longer meet the new, stricter definition. This creates a clear contradiction as Article 5.00 refers to a broad set of 'Improvements' while the defined term now encompasses a much smaller set, rendering the obligation in Article 5.00 potentially unenforceable for items that would have been licensed before.

perturbation.original_text: 3.00 Company grants to Investor, for the duration of the Option Period, a worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates) under the Patents to practice the methods therein described and claimed and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using such methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.00 Company grants to Investor, for the duration of the Option Period, a worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, revocable license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates) under the Patents to practice the methods therein described and claimed and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using such methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the license is 'irrevocable.' This change makes the license 'revocable'. This directly contradicts Article 3.03, which states that if the Option is exercised, the license becomes exclusive for a perpetual term and shall be deemed fully paid-up. A revocable license undermines the 'perpetual term' and 'fully paid-up' nature after the option is exercised, and the contradiction is also being made within the same Article 3.00 itself.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.03 If the Option is exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, the license grants set forth in Articles 3.00 and 3.01 will become exclusive to Investor for a perpetual term, shall not be subject to a licensing fee, the granted licenses in favor of the Investor shall be deemed fully paid-up, and the rights granted to Investor under Articles 3.00 and 3.01 shall include the right to grant sublicenses to Third Parties.
perturbation.justification: The change from "irrevocable" to "revocable" in Article 3.00 directly contradicts Article 3.03, which stipulates that the license becomes exclusive for a perpetual term and fully paid-up upon exercising the option. A revocable license is fundamentally incompatible with a perpetual, fully paid-up license, creating a clear and irreconcilable inconsistency within the contract. The "perpetual term" granted in Article 3.03 cannot be upheld if the license can be revoked, satisfying the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Investor will not assign to any Third Party any rights under this IP Agreement not specifically transferable by its terms without the prior written consent of Company, such consent not to be unreasonably withheld. For the avoidance of doubt and notwithstanding the foregoing: (1) such rights are assignable by Investor to an Affiliate of Investor, and (2) Investor's rights in Investor Improvements and Joint Improvements shall be freely assignable by Investor to any Person.
perturbation.changed_text: Investor will not assign to any Third Party any rights under this IP Agreement not specifically transferable by its terms without the prior written consent of Company, such consent to be granted at the Company's sole discretion. For the avoidance of doubt and notwithstanding the foregoing: (1) such rights are assignable by Investor to an Affiliate of Investor, and (2) Investor's rights in Investor Improvements and Joint Improvements shall be freely assignable by Investor to any Person.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that consent to assign rights to third parties 'not to be unreasonably withheld' by the Company. The changed text says consent 'to be granted at the Company's sole discretion'. This introduces ambiguity because 'sole discretion' suggests the Company can arbitrarily deny consent, whereas 'not to be unreasonably withheld' implies a justifiable reason is needed. This change also creates an in-text contradiction in the same Article 8.00 since the `not to be unreasonably withheld` is contradicting the `Company's sole discretion`
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.00 This IP Agreement imposes personal obligations on Investor. Investor will not assign to any Third Party any rights under this IP Agreement not specifically transferable by its terms without the prior written consent of Company, such consent to be granted at the Company's sole discretion. For the avoidance of doubt and notwithstanding the foregoing: (1) such rights are assignable by Investor to an Affiliate of Investor, and (2) Investor's rights in Investor Improvements and Joint Improvements shall be freely assignable by Investor to any Person.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated consent should not be unreasonably withheld, implying a justifiable reason is needed for denial. The changed text grants the Company 'sole discretion', implying arbitrary denial is permissible. The contradicted text contains the changed text, emphasizing the conflict between 'not to be unreasonably withheld' (original) and 'sole discretion' (changed), creating an in-text contradiction under the Ambiguities definition.
